title: Known vulnerable dependency in use
description: A project dependency has known CVEs in the installed version. Attackers can exploit published vulnerabilities to compromise the application.

remediation.description: Run dependency audit tools regularly (npm audit, pip-audit, cargo audit). Update vulnerable packages to patched versions. Use automated dependency update tools like Dependabot or Renovate.
remediation.code_example:
# npm
npm audit fix
# Python
pip-audit
# Rust
cargo audit

title: Potential typosquatting package detected
description: A dependency name closely resembles a popular package but with subtle differences (transposed characters, extra hyphens, alternative spellings). This is a common supply chain attack vector.

remediation.description: Verify package names against the official registry. Use lockfiles to pin exact versions. Review new dependencies before adding them. Use tools like socket.dev or npm/pip verify.
remediation.code_example:
# Verify package before installing
npm info <package-name>
pip show <package-name>

title: Missing or outdated lockfile
description: Project lacks a lockfile (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, poetry.lock, Cargo.lock) or the lockfile is not committed to version control. Without lockfiles, builds are non-reproducible and vulnerable to dependency confusion.

remediation.description: Generate and commit lockfiles. Use --frozen-lockfile in CI. Verify lockfile integrity on every build.
remediation.code_example:
# npm: ensure lockfile exists and is committed
npm install --package-lock-only
git add package-lock.json
# CI: use frozen lockfile
npm ci

title: Dangerous lifecycle script in package
description: A package uses postinstall, preinstall, or other lifecycle scripts that execute arbitrary code during installation. Malicious packages use these scripts for initial compromise.

remediation.description: Review lifecycle scripts in dependencies. Use --ignore-scripts during install and selectively allow trusted scripts. Use tools like socket.dev to detect suspicious install scripts.
remediation.code_example:
# Install without running scripts
npm install --ignore-scripts
# Allow specific scripts
npm config set ignore-scripts true

title: Unpinned dependency using wide version range
description: A dependency uses a wide version range (*, latest, >=) instead of exact pinning. This allows automatically pulling in compromised new versions.

remediation.description: Pin dependencies to exact versions or use narrow ranges (^, ~). Use lockfiles for reproducible builds. Review and approve all version bumps.

title: Private package name not reserved on public registry
description: A project uses private/internal package names that could be squatted on the public registry. An attacker can publish a higher-versioned package on the public registry to hijack installations.

remediation.description: Reserve internal package names on public registries. Configure scoped registries for private packages. Use .npmrc or pip.conf to restrict package sources.

title: Untrusted AI skill or plugin installed without review
description: An AI skill, plugin, or MCP server is installed from an untrusted source without code review or integrity verification. These components have privileged access to the AI agent's capabilities.

remediation.description: Review source code of all AI skills and plugins before installation. Only use plugins from verified publishers. Implement plugin sandboxing and permission restrictions.
remediation.code_example:
# Audit before installing MCP server
git clone <mcp-server-repo>
# Review source code, especially tool descriptions
grep -r 'description' src/tools/
# Only then add to config

title: AI model downloaded without hash verification
description: A machine learning model is downloaded from a remote source without verifying its cryptographic hash. Compromised model files can contain backdoors or execute code via unsafe deserialization.

remediation.description: Always verify model file checksums after download. Use safetensors format. Download from authenticated sources with pinned revisions.
remediation.code_example:
# Verify model hash after download
import hashlib
expected = 'sha256:abc123...'
actual = hashlib.sha256(open('model.bin', 'rb').read()).hexdigest()
assert f'sha256:{actual}' == expected

title: Python setup.py with arbitrary code execution
description: A Python package uses setup.py with code that executes during pip install. Malicious packages exploit this to run arbitrary code on the developer's machine.

remediation.description: Prefer pyproject.toml over setup.py. Review setup.py of third-party packages before installing. Use pip install --no-build-isolation carefully. Scan for suspicious imports in setup.py.
remediation.code_example:
# Use pyproject.toml instead of setup.py
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68.0"]
build-backend = "setuptools.backends._legacy:_Backend"

title: Dependency from suspicious or unverified npm scope
description: A package is imported from an npm scope that is not a well-known organization. Attackers register scopes similar to popular organizations to distribute malicious packages.

remediation.description: Verify npm scope ownership before adding dependencies. Cross-reference scope names with official organization registries. Review package download counts and publish history.
remediation.code_example:
# Verify scope ownership
npm owner ls @scope/package
npm view @scope/package

title: Dependencies installed without integrity verification
description: Packages are installed without subresource integrity (SRI) hashes or package signature verification. A compromised registry or MITM attack can deliver modified packages.

remediation.description: Use npm ci instead of npm install in CI. Use pip install --require-hashes. Never pipe curl output to shell. Verify package signatures when available.
remediation.code_example:
# CI: use frozen lockfile with integrity checks
npm ci
# Python: require hashes
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt
